# **Methodological and Experimental Framework for Offline Reinforcement Learning in Critical Care Sepsis Management**

## **Introduction to the Clinical State Space and Action Formulation**

The deployment of autonomous decision-making systems in critical care medicine necessitates an experimental methodology that meticulously balances algorithmic optimization with unyielding physiological reality. Sepsis, clinically defined by the Sepsis-3 consensus as a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host inflammatory response to infection, represents one of the most complex, highly stochastic, and therapeutically challenging syndromes encountered in the Intensive Care Unit (ICU).1 The core therapeutic intervention for mitigating sepsis-induced tissue hypoperfusion involves the precise, dynamic temporal titration of intravenous (IV) fluid resuscitation and vasopressor medications to restore hemodynamic stability.1 However, because prospective, online exploration by a reinforcement learning (RL) agent in a critical care environment poses unacceptable ethical and safety risks—violating the fundamental principle of clinical equipoise—the derivation of optimal treatment policies must be conducted entirely in an offline setting. Offline reinforcement learning algorithms are tasked with learning superior sequential decision-making strategies purely from retrospective, static observational datasets.4  
The methodology delineated herein establishes a highly rigorous, scientifically grounded experimental protocol for training an Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) policy on a massive, retrospective clinical dataset derived from the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care IV (MIMIC-IV) Sepsis-3 cohort.1 In constructing the environment, the longitudinal patient trajectory is mathematically modeled as a discrete-time Markov Decision Process (MDP) parameterized by a predefined 4-hour temporal resolution.6 The 4-hour step formulation is not an arbitrary hyperparameter; rather, it strikes an optimal physiological balance. It is sufficiently granular to capture the rapid, potentially fatal hemodynamic fluctuations characteristic of severe septic shock, yet sufficiently prolonged to accommodate the pharmacokinetic absorption, distribution, and elimination half-lives of administered crystalloid fluids and catecholamine-based vasopressors.3  
The state space ![][image1] is defined by a highly dimensional vector of continuous and discrete clinical variables, encapsulating patient demographics, vital signs, laboratory values, and mechanical ventilation parameters, updated at every 4-hour juncture.3 Correspondingly, the action space ![][image2] is defined as a discrete, bivariate 25-action grid. This configuration is constructed by intersecting five quantized dosing bins for intravenous fluids (representing total volume administered over the 4-hour window) with five quantized dosing bins for vasopressors (normalized to maximum norepinephrine equivalent dosing).3 This methodology strictly avoids continuous action space formulations. In the context of offline RL applied to critical care, continuous action spaces are notoriously vulnerable to uncontrolled out-of-distribution (OOD) extrapolation errors during policy extraction and evaluation. Discrete action spaces provide superior mathematical safety guarantees, facilitate exact algorithmic calculation of action probabilities, and offer much greater interpretability for subsequent clinical audits.4  
By unifying expert clinical constraints with advanced, computationally efficient machine learning theory, this framework systematically addresses the most persistent, catastrophic failure modes of healthcare-focused offline RL: reward hacking, out-of-distribution action value overestimation, and algorithmic myopia.5 The ensuing sections provide an exhaustive mathematical, statistical, and clinical justification for the specific architectural choices mandated by this protocol, moving logically from the structural engineering of the reward function and temporal discounting down to the final algorithmic extraction and validation.

| MDP Component | Clinical/Algorithmic Definition | Methodological Rationale |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **State Space (![][image1])** | 48-dimensional continuous/discrete vector (vitals, labs, demographics). | Captures comprehensive multi-organ physiological status. |
| **Action Space (![][image2])** | Discrete 25-action grid (5 IV fluid bins ![][image3] 5 Vasopressor bins). | Bounding continuous variables prevents severe OOD extrapolation. |
| **Temporal Step (![][image4])** | 4-hour resolution per sequential transition. | Aligns with vasopressor pharmacokinetics and ICU shift dynamics. |
| **Dataset Environment** | MIMIC-IV retrospective Sepsis-3 cohort. | Provides a high-fidelity, real-world observational data manifold. |

### **Reward Engineering (Ablation Strategy)**

The formulation of the mathematical reward function ![][image5] is arguably the most sensitive and consequential component of any reinforcement learning pipeline applied to medical decision-making. In standard continuous control tasks or simulated game environments, dense and highly shaped reward functions are frequently utilized to provide continuous gradient signals, guiding the agent smoothly toward an optimal policy. However, translating this engineering practice directly into the critical care domain introduces profound, potentially lethal risks, primarily in the form of specification gaming, reward hacking, and the extreme inflation of out-of-distribution action values.8 To empirically quantify and algorithmically mitigate these risks, this methodology mandates a strict, controlled ablation study comparing a Sparse (Terminal-only) reward variant against a clinically constrained Shaped (Intermediate SOFA-delta \+ Terminal) reward variant, while explicitly and forcefully rejecting overly complex "Full Shape" formulations.7  
**The Sparse Reward Variant: Mitigating Specification Bias** The Sparse reward architecture represents the most unadulterated, objective reflection of the ultimate clinical goal: patient survival. In this variant, the intermediate reward ![][image6] is strictly bounded to zero for all non-terminal time steps ![][image7] in the patient trajectory. At the terminal state ![][image8], the environment yields a singular, non-zero binary reward ![][image9], corresponding respectively to 90-day patient survival or mortality.7  
From an epidemiological and statistical perspective, the Sparse reward variant completely eliminates specification bias and human engineering artifacts. By mathematically removing all intermediate signals, the RL agent is forced to optimize purely for the ultimate ground truth of the patient outcome. The risk of the agent learning to artificially inflate intermediate physiological proxies—such as temporarily raising a patient's blood pressure while simultaneously ignoring escalating, irreversible organ failure—is entirely neutralized.7  
However, the primary vulnerability of the purely Sparse formulation is the classical reinforcement learning credit assignment problem.12 In a 4-hour step MDP formulation, an extended ICU stay can easily generate an episodic patient trajectory exceeding 50 to 100 sequential steps. If a critical, life-saving sequence of fluid boluses is appropriately administered by the agent at step ![][image10], but the positive survival reward signal ![][image11] is only achieved at step ![][image12], the underlying temporal difference learning algorithm must mathematically propagate the gradients backward through 75 environmental transitions. In a highly stochastic clinical environment characterized by hidden confounding variables and physiological noise, this prolonged gradient propagation suffers from extreme variance and severe signal attenuation. Consequently, the neural value networks frequently fail to successfully isolate and prioritize the specific causative actions that led to patient survival, resulting in under-optimized, high-entropy policies that may fail to meaningfully surpass the baseline historical clinician performance.5  
**The Shaped Reward Variant: Intermediate SOFA-Delta** To resolve the credit assignment problem without introducing dangerous clinical misalignment, the methodology introduces a highly constrained Shaped reward variant that relies exclusively on the Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score. The SOFA score is a universally recognized, heavily validated clinical index that quantitatively assesses the severity of organ dysfunction across six distinct, interconnected physiological systems: respiratory, cardiovascular, hepatic, coagulation, renal, and neurological.3  
In this Shaped variant, the intermediate reward signal is strictly defined by the temporal difference in the patient's holistic organ failure status, mathematically denoted as ![][image13]. This is expressed via the function: ![][image14] where ![][image15] and ![][image16] are scalar weighting constants empirically designed to balance the intermediate gradient flow with the terminal outcome. A positive intermediate reward is generated when the SOFA score decreases (indicating objective organ recovery), and a negative penalty is applied when the SOFA score increases (indicating clinical deterioration).3  
The utilization of ![][image13] is methodologically robust because it captures a holistic, multi-system physiological trajectory rather than isolated, easily manipulated vital signs. If a selected action temporarily stabilizes one specific organ system—for instance, administering extreme, non-physiological doses of vasopressors to maintain mean arterial pressure (MAP)—but actively destroys another system by causing acute kidney injury and profound ischemic bowel necrosis, the composite multi-organ SOFA score will ultimately rise, generating a strong immediate mathematical penalty.8 Thus, ![][image13] functions as a dense, intermediate signal that safely anchors the agent's optimization pathway, mitigating the temporal credit assignment latency while remaining tightly coupled to the overarching clinical goal of global physiological stabilization and patient survival.  
**The Rejection of "Full Shape" Architectures** While the ablation study systematically compares the Sparse and ![][image13] Shaped variants, this methodology explicitly prohibits the integration of what is termed a "Full Shape" reward architecture. A Full Shape architecture is characterized by a complex, multi-variable polynomial reward function that incorporates direct, localized penalties and bonuses for individual clinical parameters—for instance, generating continuous fractional rewards for maintaining MAP strictly above 65 mmHg, minimizing serum lactate levels, keeping urine output above 0.5 mL/kg/hr, or normalizing heart rate.3  
The rejection of Full Shape rewards is a non-negotiable safety constraint derived from the well-documented phenomenon of reward hacking (also known as myopic specification gaming) in offline RL.8 In highly dimensional, non-linear state spaces like the MIMIC-IV sepsis environment, dense multivariate rewards create fractured, highly irregular, and easily exploitable Q-value landscapes. A reinforcement learning agent, operating entirely as a mathematical optimizer stripped of clinical common sense, will rapidly identify the path of least algorithmic resistance to maximize accumulated reward.10 If maintaining a high MAP generates immediate, dense, positive rewards, the agent's policy will inevitably collapse into a behavioral local optimum: it will relentlessly recommend maximum vasopressor doses at every single time step to artificially pin the MAP above the target threshold. It will do this while entirely ignoring the silent, catastrophic physiological costs of systemic vasoconstriction, because the localized reward signal mathematically validates the action.  
Furthermore, Full Shape rewards fundamentally exacerbate the danger of Out-of-Distribution (OOD) action value inflation. In offline RL, the agent cannot interactively query the live environment to test its theories; it must estimate the value of its actions based solely on the fixed, static historical dataset.4 When complex, dense localized rewards are utilized, the neural critic network frequently overestimates the value of unobserved, extreme actions because the localized reward signal mathematically justifies them even in regions where clinical data is completely absent.5 By strictly confining the experimental ablation study to the holistic Sparse and ![][image13] variants, the methodology guarantees that the learned policies remain clinically defensive, directly preventing the algorithmic generation of myopic, physiological proxy-gaming behavior.10

| Reward Architecture | Mathematical Formulation | Intermediate Signal Focus | Methodological Status | Primary Rationale & Consequence |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Sparse** | **![][image17]** | None (Strictly Terminal) | Evaluated in Ablation | Eliminates specification bias; establishes a pure optimization baseline, but suffers from severe credit assignment latency. |
| **Shaped (![][image18]SOFA)** | **![][image19]** | Holistic Multi-Organ Status | Evaluated in Ablation | Solves credit assignment safely; prevents proxy gaming by penalizing secondary organ failure induced by aggressive treatments. |
| **Full Shape** | **![][image20]** | Localized Vital Signs & Labs | **Strictly Rejected** | Induces massive OOD reward hacking; encourages dangerous specification gaming and lethal localized optimization. |

### **MDP Formulation (The Discount Factor)**

The mathematical transformation of the raw ICU patient trajectory into a computationally tractable environment is achieved through the formal definition of the Markov Decision Process (MDP). The primary objective of the reinforcement learning agent within this MDP is to determine an optimal control policy ![][image21] that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted return from any given state, mathematically expressed as the state-value function: ![][image22] In the context of this specific methodology, the treatment of the scalar discount factor ![][image23] is subject to absolute, non-negotiable constraints. Unlike standard machine learning pipelines—particularly those derived from robotics or continuous control environments—where ![][image24] is frequently and casually treated as an empirical hyperparameter to be tuned via automated grid search, this protocol mandates that the discount factor be universally and permanently fixed at ![][image25].16  
**Mathematical Justification for the Fixed Discount Factor** The stringent rationale for fixing ![][image25] is rooted in the mathematical concept of the *effective horizon*, denoted as ![][image26]. The effective horizon defines the temporal boundary within which the agent considers future rewards to be statistically significant, effectively bounding the temporal vision of the neural networks. It is defined by the geometric series limit: ![][image27] When applying the mandated ![][image25], the calculation yields an effective horizon of ![][image28] steps.17  
This algorithmic parameter must be rigorously mapped directly back to the physical and physiological reality of the MIMIC-IV clinical environment. Because the underlying MDP is constructed with a strict 4-hour temporal resolution per state transition, an effective horizon of 100 steps corresponds exactly to 400 hours of real-world patient time, which equates to approximately 16.6 days.6  
The physiological progression, treatment, and resolution of severe sepsis and septic shock is rarely, if ever, concluded in a matter of hours. Patients admitted to the ICU with profound hemodynamic instability frequently endure prolonged, grueling clinical battles characterized by successive waves of cascading multi-organ failure, secondary nosocomial infections, and slow, highly fluctuating recovery trajectories. A 16.6-day effective horizon perfectly encapsulates the long-tail duration of a severe, complicated ICU admission. By fixing ![][image25], the value networks are mathematically forced to propagate temporal difference gradients across the entirety of the patient's likely stay. This ensures that a pharmacological action taken by the agent on Day 1 is intrinsically and relentlessly evaluated against its ultimate potential to cause mortality or facilitate healthy discharge on Day 15\.  
**Algorithmic Myopia and the Clinical Danger of Temporal Discounting** In the broader offline RL literature, researchers frequently attempt to artificially lower the discount factor (e.g., to ![][image29], ![][image30], or even ![][image31]) to ease the algorithmic learning process and stabilize neural network convergence.18 Lowering ![][image24] mathematically dampens the recursive variance inherent to the Bellman operator. Because offline RL relies entirely on a fixed, static dataset, the temporal difference target values ![][image32] are highly susceptible to cascading error propagation. A lower ![][image24] exponentially decays the influence of future states, aggressively cutting off the error propagation and creating a much smoother, easier-to-fit loss landscape for the critic networks.  
However, utilizing a lowered discount factor to solve underlying algorithmic instability is clinically catastrophic in the context of critical care medicine. Consider the profound mathematical and clinical implications of tuning the parameter down to ![][image30]. The effective horizon instantly and violently collapses to ![][image33] steps. At 4 hours per step, the agent's temporal vision is restricted to a mere 40 hours.  
An RL agent operating with a 40-hour horizon is fundamentally blind to the long-term physiological consequences of its actions. This induces severe, potentially lethal algorithmic myopia.8 If an agent is temporally discounted to ignore any physiological state beyond 40 hours, it will invariably learn a policy that aggressively optimizes for immediate hemodynamic stabilization at the total cost of eventual patient survival. For example, the myopic agent will learn to administer massive, continuous, toxic doses of vasopressors, because these actions rapidly and reliably correct hypotensive states within the immediate 40-hour window. The temporally discounted agent is mathematically incapable of recognizing that the resultant profound peripheral ischemia, induced by the vasoconstriction, will eventually lead to necrotic bowel syndrome, complete renal failure, and unavoidable patient mortality on Day 7 (168 hours later).9  
By strictly prohibiting the treatment of ![][image24] as a tunable hyperparameter and irrevocably locking it at ![][image34] 16, the methodology enforces long-term clinical safety. The computational challenges associated with fitting complex Q-values over a highly stochastic 100-step horizon will not be bypassed by artificially blinding the agent; instead, they will be solved computationally via the deployment of the sophisticated Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) architecture and a rigorously staged, highly controlled hyperparameter optimization protocol.

### **Compute-Efficient IQL Hyperparameter Sweep**

To successfully navigate the complex, high-dimensional MIMIC-IV dataset without succumbing to the fatal extrapolation errors prevalent in offline RL, the methodology relies exclusively on the Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) algorithm.4 Traditional offline actor-critic algorithms, such as Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) or Bear, attempt to mitigate OOD action inflation by explicitly querying the policy network for unseen actions and mathematically penalizing their resulting Q-values during training.5 While effective in theory, this explicit penalization process is computationally burdensome and highly sensitive to hyperparameter scaling. In sparse medical datasets, it frequently leads to policies that collapse into overly pessimistic, rigid behavioral cloning of the historical data, failing to provide any meaningful policy improvement.5  
IQL completely bypasses the necessity of querying unseen actions.4 The central, elegant innovation of IQL lies in treating the optimal state-value function ![][image35] as a random variable determined by the action distribution, and subsequently estimating the upper expectile of this distribution.4 This in-sample learning approach allows the algorithm to implicitly deduce the value of the best actions available within the dataset manifold without ever directly evaluating actions outside the established dataset boundaries.4  
However, the reliance on critical, mathematically sensitive hyperparameters—specifically the expectile (![][image36]) and the inverse temperature (![][image37])—presents a complex optimization challenge.16 A conventional, naive grid search over all variables simultaneously (including learning rates, expectiles, and temperatures) is both computationally inefficient and methodologically flawed.25 In actor-critic architectures like IQL, the learning dynamics of the value networks (the critic) and the policy network (the actor) are inherently misaligned.25 Attempting to optimize them simultaneously leads to chaotic gradient updates where an unstable actor generates poor advantage estimates, which in turn corrupts the extraction landscape. To establish maximum clinical validity, this methodology enforces a stringent, 3-Stage Compute-Efficient Hyperparameter Sweep Protocol that mathematically decouples the establishment of the Q-value geometry from the final policy extraction process.25  
**Stage 1 (Geometry): Establishing the Asymmetric Loss Landscape** The primary objective of Stage 1 is to perfectly map the state-action value landscape without interference from a fluctuating policy network. In IQL, the state-value network ![][image38] and the action-value Q-network ![][image39] are entirely independent of the actor network ![][image40] during their core optimization phases.4 The objective function for the value network utilizes an asymmetric ![][image41] loss governed by the expectile parameter ![][image42]: ![][image43] where the expectile loss is defined as ![][image44].  
During this stage, the base neural architecture learning rates are strictly frozen. The critic learning rate (critic\_lr) and value learning rate (value\_lr) are held constant at ![][image45], utilizing the Adam optimizer.16 The actor learning rate is also provisionally frozen at a baseline ![][image46].16  
With the learning rates stabilized, the protocol executes a targeted sweep across the two variables that define the geometric bounds of the value landscape: the expectile ![][image36] and the temperature ![][image37]. The expectile governs the degree of off-policy optimism. As ![][image47], the algorithm mimics strictly on-policy SARSA; as ![][image48], it approximates pure off-policy Q-learning.24 In the stochastic, high-variance environment of ICU sepsis, excessive optimism leads to catastrophic overestimation. Therefore, the sweep is strictly bounded to clinically defensive values: ![][image49].16 Similarly, the temperature parameter ![][image37], which controls the strictness of the advantage weighting during extraction, is bounded to moderate slopes: ![][image50].16  
This specific grid is executed across 3 independent random seeds to control for stochastic network initialization. By freezing the learning rates, the methodology mathematically guarantees that any divergence in offline policy evaluation metrics is entirely attributable to the fundamental conservatism of the value geometry, establishing a highly robust foundation for the subsequent extraction.  
**Stage 2 (Extraction): Conservative Policy Extraction via AWBC** Once the optimal geometric configuration of ![][image51] is empirically identified via the Stage 1 evaluations, these parameters are permanently frozen. Stage 2 shifts focus entirely to the mechanics of the Advantage-Weighted Behavioral Cloning (AWBC) extraction.4 The actor loss function is defined as: ![][image52]  
The primary variable tuned in this phase is purely the actor learning rate (actor\_lr). The methodology mandates a sweep testing conservative learning rates relative to the critic/value networks, specifically comparing a baseline ![][image46] against highly restricted updates like ![][image53].25  
The clinical and mathematical justification for a heavily restricted actor learning rate is paramount. In offline RL applied to retrospective, highly fractured medical data, the estimated Q-values, regardless of the expectile conservatism applied, will inevitably contain localized regions of high error and noise due to dataset sparsity.5 If the actor network is permitted to update with an aggressive, rapid learning rate, it will quickly overfit to these erroneous spikes in the Q-value landscape, manifesting as highly dangerous, erratic clinical actions upon deployment. A significantly slower, conservative actor learning rate ensures that the neural network performs a smooth, generalized projection over the advantage-weighted manifold.5 It forces the actor to identify broad, statistically verified regions of successful treatment (such as sustained moderate IV fluid administration over several consecutive time steps) rather than chasing isolated, anomalous, and potentially lethal peaks in the historical data.  
**Stage 3 (Final Execution): Statistical Robustness**  
Following the precise decoupling of the geometry optimization (Stage 1\) and the extraction parameterization (Stage 2), the framework yields a single, globally optimized hyperparameter configuration that is clinically defensive, resistant to overestimation, and smoothly extracted.  
In Stage 3, this winning configuration is subjected to a final, rigorous execution protocol. The model is trained identically across 5 new, independent random seeds. Offline RL is notoriously susceptible to high intra-algorithmic variance; an algorithm that performs exceptionally on Seed 1 may completely collapse on Seed 2 due to minor variations in the neural network initialization interacting with the boundaries of the static offline dataset.5 By averaging the target policy performance and calculating tight, mathematically rigorous 95% confidence intervals across 5 independent seeds, the methodology ensures robust, reproducible statistical reporting that can withstand the most intense peer and clinical review.

| Optimization Protocol | Algorithmic Objective | Tuned Hyperparameters | Frozen Hyperparameters | Replication Strategy |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Stage 1 (Geometry)** | Map the asymmetric ![][image41] loss landscape; establish upper expectile bounds. | Expectile ![][image49], Temp ![][image50] | critic\_lr (![][image54]), value\_lr (![][image54]), actor\_lr (![][image55]) | 3 independent seeds to isolate geometry variance. |
| **Stage 2 (Extraction)** | Determine smooth projection rate over the AWBC behavioral cloning manifold. | actor\_lr ![][image56] | Winning ![][image51] config, critic\_lr, value\_lr | Evaluated on the single best seed from Stage 1\. |
| **Stage 3 (Validation)** | Establish definitive statistical robustness and absolute confidence intervals. | None (Execute finalized winning configuration) | All parameters permanently frozen to optimal values. | 5 independent seeds to ensure reproducibility. |

### **Evaluation Protocol**

The culmination of the experimental methodology is the stringent Offline Policy Evaluation (OPE) framework. Because evaluating the newly derived IQL policy via direct clinical deployment or prospective A/B testing on live patients is ethically and legally impossible without extensive prerequisite validation 26, the mathematical measurement of the policy's efficacy must be conducted purely on a strictly held-out test cohort of the MIMIC-IV dataset.27  
Evaluating a target evaluation policy ![][image57] using a static dataset generated by a different, unknown behavior policy ![][image58] (the historical ICU clinicians) introduces massive statistical challenges.19 Basic evaluation methods like Direct Method (DM) modeling often suffer from extreme bias, while pure Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW) methods suffer from mathematically unbounded variance due to the likelihood ratio calculations. To achieve a comprehensive, unassailable assessment of the agent's absolute and relative performance, the protocol utilizes a dual-metric validation strategy combining Fitted Q-Evaluation (FQE) and Weighted Importance Sampling (WIS).27  
**Fitted Q-Evaluation (FQE): The Primary Minimax Estimator** Fitted Q-Evaluation (FQE) serves as the primary metric for internal model selection, hyperparameter ranking across the sweep stages, and absolute performance estimation.27 FQE is a model-free, regression-based tool that estimates the value of the evaluation policy ![][image57] by algorithmically reconstructing the action-value function directly from the historical test data.27  
Mathematically, FQE applies an iterative fixed-point reduction based on the Bellman equation, crucially removing the maximum operator utilized in active Q-learning to evaluate the specific policy.30 Given the held-out transition dataset ![][image59], FQE iteratively solves the supervised learning problem: ![][image60] until formal convergence is achieved.30  
The profound advantage of FQE in the context of critical care is its numerical stability, sample efficiency, and minimax optimality.29 Unlike importance sampling methods, FQE does not explicitly rely on calculating the exact action likelihood ratios between the RL agent and the historical clinicians. This insulates it entirely from the extreme, exponential variance caused by long-horizon trajectories (such as the 100-step effective horizon dictated by ![][image25]).33 Extensive empirical studies within healthcare RL have consistently, unequivocally demonstrated that FQE produces the most robust and accurate ranking of candidate policies, exhibiting the lowest mean squared error when predicting actual target policy returns in highly complex, stochastic environments like sepsis management.3 Consequently, the outcomes of the Stage 1 and Stage 2 hyperparameter sweeps are adjudicated primarily on their resultant FQE scores.  
**Weighted Importance Sampling (WIS): The Unbiased Sanity Check** While FQE provides a highly stable ranking metric, it is technically classified in statistical literature as a biased estimator because it relies entirely on the internal parameterization and projection norms of its own neural function approximators.27 If the FQE neural networks suffer from systemic model misspecification regarding the underlying dataset dynamics, the resulting evaluation could quietly overestimate the safety and efficacy of the learned policy.  
To provide an absolute statistical safeguard against FQE bias, the methodology incorporates Weighted Importance Sampling (WIS) as a mandatory secondary validation metric.3 WIS is a trajectory-based OPE method that estimates the value of the target policy by re-weighting the actual, ground-truth historical returns recorded in the dataset.27 The re-weighting is determined by the cumulative product of importance weights (the likelihood ratio) across the episodic sequence: ![][image61] Because this sequential multiplication can cause the variance of the estimate to explode over a 100-step trajectory, standard Importance Sampling is mathematically unstable.27 Weighted Importance Sampling mitigates this by normalizing the estimates by the sum of the trajectory weights across the dataset, yielding an estimator that, while maintaining higher variance than FQE, provides a fundamentally unbiased representation of policy performance directly anchored to real patient outcomes.6  
**Synthesis of the Evaluation Framework** By simultaneously deploying both FQE and WIS on the rigorously segregated held-out MIMIC-IV test cohort, the methodology ensures clean, undeniable algorithmic validation.2 FQE provides the statistical power and low variance required to confidently declare quantitative victory over historical baseline clinician performance (or random behavioral cloning metrics).33 Simultaneously, the WIS estimates act as a critical transparency mechanism, highlighting overlapping confidence intervals and mathematically confirming that the robust performance indicated by FQE is grounded in actual historical survival trajectories, not merely an artifact of internal function approximator bias.3  
When executed chronologically—from the precise, clinically defended ablation of the reward signal, the strict physiological anchoring of the temporal discount factor to prevent algorithmic myopia, and the computationally staged decoupling of the IQL geometry from policy extraction, down to the dual-metric OPE validation—this methodology constructs an impervious, scientifically unassailable framework. It effectively neutralizes the primary dangers of Q-value overestimation and myopic reward hacking, facilitating the generation of a reinforcement learning policy that is mathematically rigorous, statistically verifiable, and uncompromisingly anchored to the safety dynamics of ICU sepsis management.
